elements.content: A showcase of StoryPackager layout modes: floating images, story-absolute anchoring, wrap variants, and multi-page pagination. Each section demonstrates one specific feature of the engine.

children.content: Story-absolute images are pinned at fixed story-local coordinates, independent of the text flow. The burgundy rectangle at upper right was declared as the very first child of this story element and was registered in the SpatialMap during Pass 1 — before a single word of body text was placed. All text from the story’s origin automatically pours around it into the remaining column width to its left.

When the story spans multiple pages, any absolute image that straddles the page break is moved entirely to the next page. Publication-quality layout demands clean image boundaries: no clipping, no partial rendering mid-image.

The right-aligned float anchors at the text cursor the moment its element is encountered in the child list. Subsequent lines pour into the remaining column to its left — the classic magazine treatment, water parting around a stone. The lineLayoutResolver is called once per line, querying the SpatialMap for available horizontal intervals at that line’s Y coordinate in story space.

Once the cursor clears the foot of the float, the full column width is restored. The transition is seamless: the same text box holds lines of varying widths, driven by per-line data stored in the _lineWidths array. No element boundary coincides with the float boundary, and no special-casing is needed in the renderer. The resolver tracks state in a local closure and relies on wrapRichSegments calling it with strictly ascending line indices.

An amber square occupies the left margin here, with body text filling the wider right column. PageMaker pioneered this runaround layout in 1985, bringing professional typesetting to the Macintosh. The SpatialMap knows only rectangles and their wrap modes; the rest is interval arithmetic — subtract the obstacle’s X range from the available column width and assign the remainder to each line.

The slate-gray banner above demonstrates top-bottom wrap: no text runs alongside it; the full column clears above and below. The cursor advances past the image bottom before any text is placed. Wide pull quotes, full-width dividers, and chapter ornaments are the natural candidates for this mode.

A centered float carves two equal intervals from each line it occupies. The resolver fills the left interval first, then immediately continues into the right interval at the same baseline — a single unbroken token stream flowing around both flanks of the obstacle simultaneously. The text narrows on each side for the lines that coincide with the ornament, then springs back to full measure once the float is cleared.
After the float bottom is cleared the full measure is restored, and the layout engine behaves identically to a flat, obstacle-free column for all remaining lines in the paragraph.

Setting wrap to none makes the image purely decorative — it registers no obstacle in the SpatialMap, so not a single character of text is reflowed. Body copy renders at full column width and the image overlaps it visually. Boxes are painted in document order, so depth is controlled by child order in the story.

Pagination in the StoryPackager follows a split-and-resume pattern. When available page height is exhausted mid-story, the packager emits a FrozenStoryPackager for the current-page content and creates a new StoryPackager for the continuation. Any floats that straddle the break become carry-over obstacles, registered at Y 0 on the next page so text continues to wrap around their remaining height correctly. Story-absolute images past the break are re-included in the continuation children and re-anchored at their story coordinates adjusted by the split offset.

The architecture keeps all story-local coordinates intact across the split. A float anchored on page one whose bottom extends onto page two simply reappears at the top of page two with its remaining height, and text wraps around that carry-over obstacle just as it did on the first page. No special cases, no re-layout of previously rendered content — the SpatialMap and the stateful resolver handle it transparently, treating the carry-over as just another registered obstacle at Y 0.
